title: Second jurisdiction tenant-rights pack
context: After the first pilot jurisdiction's tenant-rights content is stable and delivered (M6), Know-Your-Rights can expand to a second jurisdiction. Each jurisdiction requires its own source vetting (edict-of-government reuse terms), a licensed attorney admitted in that jurisdiction, and per-jurisdiction sourcing — there are no shortcuts to accuracy and currency. Tenant law varies significantly by jurisdiction, and content from jurisdiction A cannot be re-used for jurisdiction B. This task is backlog: not started until M6 is complete.
objective: Produce the full source-vetted, schema-valid, attorney-reviewed structured tenant-rights dataset for a second confirmed jurisdiction, following the same process as the pilot: source vetting with recorded reuse terms, content drafting with primary-source citations to the second jurisdiction's law, content-lint suite passing, and licensed attorney sign-off (admitted in the second jurisdiction) before delivery.
acceptanceCriteria: Second jurisdiction confirmed and selected using the same scoring criteria as task 000 (edict-of-government reusability, access-to-justice need, attorney availability, legal complexity/stability, partner plausibility)
Full source vetting completed for the second jurisdiction: all candidate sources assessed, reuse terms verified and recorded, attorney sign-off on source list
Tenant-rights explainers produced for the second jurisdiction covering the six core topics; all content primary-source-cited to the second jurisdiction's law (not the first jurisdiction)
All content passes the full content-lint suite (task 004) and is schema-valid per task 003
Licensed-attorney sign-off recorded for the second jurisdiction content (attorney admitted in the second jurisdiction, not the first); sign-off version-scoped
Structured dataset records produced for the second jurisdiction and merged into the dataset export (task 011 extended); CI validates both jurisdictions
Sequenced after M6 is complete; no content from the first jurisdiction is repurposed for the second without explicit per-claim review and re-citation
output: A CC-BY-4.0 structured tenant-rights dataset for a second confirmed jurisdiction — source-vetted, schema-valid, primary-source-cited to that jurisdiction's law, lint-clean, and licensed-attorney-signed-off — expanding the Know-Your-Rights commons to a second jurisdiction.
